title: Network Effects

content.statement: More users make the product more valuable.
content.question: If your existing users vanished tomorrow, would the product be exactly as valuable to the ones who stayed, or did their presence make it better?
sop: Identify what a new user contributes to the experience of every other user, content, liquidity, connections, or data.
Test whether the product is meaningfully worse with 10 users than with 1,000, if there's no difference, you don't have a network effect yet.
Design one feature that explicitly surfaces other users' activity to new ones (reviews, activity feed, matches, community).
Count your current active users, and rate how much value each additional user adds to the experience, 1-10 (see calculation, page 5).
Protect early users' experience even while chasing growth, a network effect dies if the early adopters leave.
questions: If your existing users vanished tomorrow, would the product be exactly as valuable to the ones who stayed, or did their presence make it better?
What's the one thing early users get from later users joining, more content, faster matches, a bigger community, that new users can't get from you alone?
Are you still designing your product to work well with 10 users, or does it require hundreds to feel valuable?

definitions.def: When a product becomes more valuable as more people use it.
In a marketplace, how fast a user gets what they came for. Slow matching = pre-value churn. Speed of satisfaction is the metric.

content.equation_example: Value scales roughly with Users squared, a relative index, not literal dollars. 10 users gives value index 100; 100 users gives value index 10,000. Rising faster than linear means real network effects; if value only grows in a straight line with users, it's just user growth, not a network effect.
content.closing_statement: Design for what users give each other, not you.

caption:
Principle Nº 174: Network Effects

More users make the product more valuable.

Ask yourself: If your existing users vanished tomorrow, would the product be exactly as valuable to the ones who stayed, or did their presence make it better?
